class_schedule.topic: The Podcast Landscape and Audio Content Strategy
learning_objectives: Analyze the current podcast ecosystem including platforms, monetization, and audience behavior
Define a podcast concept with clear positioning, target audience, and format
Distinguish between common podcast formats and their production requirements
readings: Berry, Podcasting: A Practical Guide, Ch. 1-3
Edison Research, 'The Infinite Dial' (latest annual podcast listening report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Podcasting is the most intimate digital medium. A listener gives you their ears, often for 30-60 minutes, frequently while alone. This level of attention and trust is unmatched by any other channel. This course teaches you to produce professional audio content that earns and rewards that trust.
key_concepts: Podcast market overview: 4+ million podcasts, but only a fraction publish consistently; opportunity remains enormous
Podcast formats: solo monologue, interview/conversational, narrative/storytelling, panel, hybrid
Positioning and differentiation: finding a unique angle in a crowded market
Audience personas for audio: commuters, exercisers, multitaskers, and how context shapes listening
Audio content beyond podcasts: audiograms, voice search optimization, audio social (Twitter Spaces), internal corporate audio
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing three successful podcasts from different genres, deconstructing their format, production quality, hosting style, and audience engagement strategy to identify what makes each work
setup_requirements: Podcast listening app (Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or Overcast)
USB microphone or XLR microphone with interface
Digital Audio Workstation: Audacity, GarageBand, or Adobe Audition

review.value: Defining who the podcast is for, what problem it solves or need it fills, and what makes it different from the hundreds of other podcasts on the same topic; specificity of audience and angle is the primary differentiator

The unique quality of audio content where the listener perceives the host as speaking directly to them, creating a parasocial relationship and trust that exceeds visual media; this intimacy is a strength but also a responsibility

class_schedule.topic: Recording Studio Setup and Microphone Technique
learning_objectives: Set up a home or portable recording environment that minimizes unwanted noise and reflections
Apply proper microphone technique for consistent, professional vocal recording
Troubleshoot common recording problems: plosives, sibilance, room echo, and electrical noise

lecture_notes.introduction: Your recording environment and microphone technique determine 80% of your final audio quality. No amount of post-production can fix a bad recording. Investing time in getting your recording right at the source saves hours of editing and produces superior results.
key_concepts: Room treatment on a budget: absorption panels, blankets, closet recording, portable vocal shields
Microphone polar patterns: cardioid (rejects rear sound), omnidirectional (captures everything), figure-8 (two-person setup)
Mic technique: distance (6-8 inches for warmth), angle (slightly off-axis to reduce plosives), consistency
Gain staging: setting input levels for clean recording with headroom (-12dB average, -6dB peaks)
Common problems and solutions: pop filters for plosives, de-essers for sibilance, noise gates for ambient sound
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a recording station from scratch in the classroom, recording the same passage with different microphone distances, angles, and room treatments, comparing results on studio monitors
setup_requirements: Acoustic treatment materials or improvised alternatives

review.value: Setting the input level so that the average signal sits around -12dB with peaks reaching -6dB, providing enough headroom to avoid clipping while maintaining a strong signal-to-noise ratio
The boost in bass frequencies that occurs when a sound source is very close to a directional microphone; controlled distance creates warmth, but too close creates boomy, muddy audio

learning_objectives: Prepare for and conduct a podcast interview that yields compelling, usable content
Apply active listening and follow-up questioning techniques during live recording
Manage technical challenges during remote and in-person interviews
readings: Gross, The Art of the Interview, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: The interview is the core production unit of the most popular podcast format. A great interview is not a conversation; it is a guided exploration where the host's preparation and listening skills determine whether the guest reveals something the audience has never heard before.
key_concepts: Research depth: knowing your guest well enough to ask questions others have not asked
Question design: open-ended questions, follow-up probes, the power of silence as a prompt
Active listening: hearing what is actually said vs. waiting for your turn to speak
Remote interview recording: double-ender technique, platform options (Riverside, Zencastr, Zoom)
Managing difficult moments: redirecting rambling guests, handling controversial answers, technical failures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live interview practice in pairs: each student prepares and conducts a 10-minute interview with a classmate, followed by playback and critique of questioning technique, flow, and audio quality

review.value: A remote interview technique where both host and guest record their own audio locally on high-quality equipment, then the files are combined in post-production, avoiding the quality degradation of recording over internet audio

After a guest gives a surface-level answer, remaining silent for 3-5 seconds often prompts them to go deeper, filling the silence with the more thoughtful, personal, or revealing answer that makes great podcast content

learning_objectives: Edit a podcast episode using professional post-production workflow
Apply noise reduction, EQ, compression, and limiting to polish vocal audio
Mix multiple audio sources (voice, music, sound effects) at appropriate levels

lecture_notes.introduction: Post-production is where a raw recording becomes a polished listening experience. The goal is not to make the audio sound processed, but to make it sound effortless: clear, consistent, and comfortable to listen to for extended periods.
key_concepts: Editing workflow: remove errors, cut dead space, tighten pacing, smooth transitions
Noise reduction: spectral analysis, noise print capture, reduction threshold and amount settings
EQ for voice: high-pass filter to remove rumble, presence boost (2-5kHz) for clarity, de-essing (6-8kHz)
Compression: evening out volume differences, setting threshold, ratio, attack, and release for natural sound
Loudness normalization: targeting -16 LUFS for podcasts (Spotify/Apple standard)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Taking a raw 20-minute interview recording through the complete post-production pipeline: editing for content and pacing, noise reduction, EQ, compression, adding intro/outro music, and exporting at -16 LUFS
setup_requirements: DAW with noise reduction capability (Audacity, Adobe Audition, or Hindenburg)
Raw interview recording from previous session

review.value: Loudness Units relative to Full Scale: the broadcast loudness standard; podcasts target -16 LUFS (Spotify, Apple Podcasts) to ensure consistent volume across episodes and between different podcasts

An EQ setting that removes all frequencies below a set point (typically 80-100Hz for voice), eliminating low-frequency rumble from handling noise, traffic vibration, and air conditioning without affecting speech clarity

learning_objectives: Set up podcast hosting and distribute to all major platforms via RSS
Develop a launch strategy that maximizes initial visibility and reviews
Create audiograms and promotional content to drive discovery on social media
readings: Berry, Podcasting: A Practical Guide, Ch. 10-12

lecture_notes.introduction: A great podcast that nobody can find is just an audio file on a hard drive. Distribution and growth strategy are as important as production quality. Understanding how podcast discovery works and how to promote your show is essential for building an audience.
key_concepts: RSS fundamentals: how the podcast RSS feed delivers episodes to all platforms simultaneously
Hosting platforms: Buzzsprout, Libsyn, Anchor, and what to evaluate when choosing
Launch strategy: pre-launch buzz, episode bank (3-5 episodes at launch), reviews and ratings push
Podcast SEO: episode titles, show notes, transcripts, and keyword optimization for search and discovery
Audiograms: creating short video clips with waveform animations for social media promotion
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a podcast on Buzzsprout: uploading an episode, writing optimized show notes, submitting to Apple Podcasts and Spotify, and creating three audiograms for social promotion using Headliner
setup_requirements: Podcast hosting account (Buzzsprout free tier or equivalent)

review.value: Really Simple Syndication: the XML file that contains your podcast metadata and episode list, submitted once to each platform (Apple, Spotify, etc.), which then automatically checks for new episodes
A short video clip combining a static image or waveform animation with a podcast audio excerpt and captions, designed for social media promotion where audio-only content does not stop the scroll

questions.question: Explain gain staging for podcast recording. What target levels should you aim for, and what happens if you set the gain too high or too low?
questions.answer: Gain staging is setting the input level of your microphone for optimal recording quality. Target -12dB average level with peaks reaching no higher than -6dB. If gain is set too high, the audio clips (distorts) when the speaker gets louder, producing harsh distortion that cannot be fixed in post-production. If gain is set too low, the signal is weak relative to the noise floor, meaning when you amplify the recording later, you also amplify the background noise, resulting in a hissy, unprofessional sound.

questions.question: What is the proximity effect, and how does it affect microphone technique for podcast recording?
questions.answer: The proximity effect is a boost in bass frequencies that occurs when a sound source is very close to a directional (cardioid) microphone. For podcast recording, this means speaking very close to the mic creates a warm, bass-heavy sound that many listeners find pleasant, but too close creates a boomy, muddy quality that obscures clarity. The recommended distance of 6-8 inches balances warmth with clarity. Maintaining consistent distance is critical because moving closer and further during recording creates audible volume and tonal shifts that are difficult to fix in post-production.

questions.question: Describe the purpose of compression in podcast audio post-production. What do the threshold and ratio settings control?
questions.answer: Compression evens out the volume differences in a podcast recording by reducing the dynamic range: making quiet parts louder relative to loud parts. The threshold setting determines the volume level above which compression begins (e.g., -20dB means only audio louder than -20dB is affected). The ratio setting determines how much the audio above the threshold is reduced (e.g., a 3:1 ratio means audio 3dB above the threshold is reduced to 1dB above). This creates a more consistent listening experience where the audience does not need to constantly adjust their volume.

questions.question: What is an RSS feed in podcast distribution, and why is it preferable to upload episodes to a hosting platform rather than directly to Apple Podcasts or Spotify?
questions.answer: An RSS (Really Simple Syndication) feed is an XML file hosted by your podcast hosting platform that contains your show's metadata, episode list, and audio file URLs. You submit this feed URL once to each podcast platform, and they automatically check it for new episodes. Using a hosting platform with RSS is preferable because: (1) You upload once and reach all platforms simultaneously. (2) You retain control of your content and can switch hosting providers by redirecting the RSS feed. (3) If you upload directly to one platform, you lose portability and must manage each platform separately. (4) Your hosting platform provides analytics and bandwidth management.

major_assignments.title: Podcast Pilot: Three-Episode Mini-Series
major_assignments.description: Create a three-episode podcast mini-series on a topic of your choice. Develop the show concept, produce all three episodes with professional audio quality, design cover art and branding, and prepare the series for distribution. At least one episode must include an interview segment.
objectives: Develop a podcast concept with clear positioning, audience, and format
Produce three complete episodes demonstrating recording and editing mastery
Conduct at least one professional podcast interview
Prepare the series for distribution with all required metadata and assets
deliverables: Show concept document (2-3 pages) defining audience, format, positioning, and episode plan
Three produced episodes (15-25 minutes each) edited to -16 LUFS
Show notes with timestamps and links for each episode
Distribution-ready RSS feed or hosting platform upload

criteria: Show concept is clearly defined with specific audience and unique positioning
Format is consistent and appropriate for the topic
Episode planning demonstrates thoughtful content arc across three episodes
Branding is professional and cohesive
Recording is clean with minimal background noise
Vocal audio is properly EQ'd and compressed
Music and sound elements are mixed at appropriate levels
Episodes are normalized to -16 LUFS
Content is engaging, well-structured, and serves the target audience
Interview demonstrates preparation, active listening, and follow-up skills
Hosting style is confident, clear, and appropriate for the format
Episodes have clear intros, transitions, and conclusions
All metadata is complete and accurate
Show notes include timestamps and relevant links
Cover art meets platform specifications
Series is uploaded to hosting platform and submitted to at least one directory
Strong concept with clear positioning and audience definition
Professional audio quality throughout all three episodes
Compelling content with skilled interviewing and hosting
Distribution-ready package meeting all platform requirements

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on audio production concepts and podcast strategy

weekly_production_exercises.description: Weekly recording and editing exercises building toward the major project

major_assignment.description: Three-episode podcast mini-series demonstrating complete production pipeline

midterm_production.description: Mid-semester audio production piece showcasing recording and editing skills

final_portfolio.description: Final audio portfolio including best work and production growth reflection

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all studio sessions and peer review workshops. Recording studio time is limited and scheduled; missed sessions cannot be rescheduled. More than 2 absences result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late production exercises lose 10% per day. Podcast episodes submitted late lose 15% per day since peer listening schedules depend on timely delivery.
course_policies.collaboration: Technical assistance (holding a boom mic, monitoring levels) during recording is encouraged. All editorial and production decisions for graded work must be individual. Sharing raw audio files or project files is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All audio content must be original recordings. Music must be royalty-free or properly licensed. Interview subjects must consent to recording and publication. Fabricating interviews or presenting pre-existing audio as original is academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a USB or XLR microphone, headphones, a DAW (Audacity is free and sufficient), and a podcast hosting account (free tier). The campus recording studio is available by reservation for scheduled recording sessions.
